summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Releasing documents from current matter to be used in matters in other jurisdictions - Parenting arrangements reopened due to family violence and sexual assault allegations – Release of material to parties for use in criminal, family violence order, and disciplinary proceedings – Material produced under subpoena, disclosure, and Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) s 67ZBE –– Party is self-represented in other proceedings - Safeguards for release of material – Special circumstances exist.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

GILL J

The proceedings in this court relate to the parenting arrangements for the parties’ three children in circumstances where consent orders entered into in October 2022 provided for a substantially shared arrangement, but where the parenting arrangements are now reopened following family violence allegations made by each of the parents against the other, and in particular, following sexual assault allegations made by the mother against the father.
A history of the proceedings and account of the parties’ competing cases is set out in the interim judgment of Judge Costigan of 20 January 2026. The proceedings have now been transferred to Division 1 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia.
The father now faces criminal proceedings in the Supreme Court of the ACT in relation to sexual assault offences alleged to have been perpetrated against the mother in 2017, prior to separation and prior to the earlier consent orders.
Each of the parents has now pursued family violence order proceedings in the ACT Magistrates Court, the father with a pending final application against the mother, the mother with a Special Interim Family Violence Order that will remain in place pending a final hearing to take place following the resolution of criminal charges against the father. Depending upon where the truth of the matter lies each of the parents asserts that this, like the sexual assault issue, is an aspect of the family violence perpetrated upon them by the other, as a form of systems abuse.
The father, a member of F Organisation, currently faces disciplinary proceedings that may result in the termination of his employment. Those proceedings centre on the sexual assault allegations as a breach of the code of conduct that binds him as an employee of F Organisation.
The current application relates to the release of material filed or produced in these proceedings for use in each of the three sets of proceedings identified above.
The parties were in partial agreement about the release of documents for use in the various proceedings. They provided a table setting out the classes of documents for which one or other sought permission, and the use of that material that was agreed or disagreed. That table is annexed to this judgment.

PRINCIPLES

Before turning to the material itself it is useful to identify the principles at play. First, r 15.26 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (“the rule”) applies to all of the documents identified below, and is in the following terms:
15.26 Use of documents or information obtained in proceedings
(1) The court may grant permission for a person to use a document or information obtained in proceedings for another purpose, including in proceedings before another court or tribunal, if the person can satisfy the court that special circumstances exist to permit the person to use the document or information for that other purpose.

(2) In this rule:
document includes, but is not limited to, the following:

(a) a document filed in a proceeding in accordance with these Rules or an order;

(b) a document produced in accordance with a disclosure obligation or a subpoena;

(c) a document admitted into evidence in a proceeding;

(d) a document provided to the court in accordance with section 67ZBD or 67ZBE of the Family Law Act;

(e) a family report or single expert report;

(f) reasons for judgment whether anonymised or not.
The rule, protective as it is in respect of the privacy of the litigants, children and of those who produce material to the court, calls, in considering release, for focus on the existence of “special circumstances” that justify the external use of the documents. In this case, the key circumstance for each use is the nature and subject matter of the proceedings in which it is proposed that the material be used. The criminal proceedings, the family violence order proceedings, and the disciplinary proceedings each involve the sexual assault allegations that are set within a relationship otherwise beset with family violence allegations. Each of the proceedings are capable of forming a component of “special circumstances” due to the consequences inherent to each, which perhaps at their lowest level are the disciplinary proceedings that carry the prospect of a loss of livelihood for the father.
The rule sits within the context of the general law about the use of document produced in and as a result of proceedings. The general position, described in Hearne v Street [2008] HCA 36 (“Hearne v Street”) at [96] is that:
Where one party to litigation is compelled, either by reason of a rule of court, or by reason of a specific order of the court, or otherwise, to disclose documents or information, the party obtaining the disclosure cannot, without the leave of the court, use it for any purpose other than that for which it was given unless it is received into evidence.
It may be seen that the rule is cast more broadly than the restriction contained in Hearne v Street, for example, extending even to documents that have been received into evidence.
In a manner reflected by the terms of the rule, the Hearne v Street obligations are subject to relief in justifying circumstances, in a manner illustrative of the reasoning that will identify relevant “special circumstances” under the rule. In Hermann & Hermann (No 2) [2014] FamCA 587 Macmillan J neatly summarised those principles from [15]:
A party may be released from that implied obligation if there are special circumstances for doing so. Wilcox J in Re Springfield Nominees Pty Ltd [1992] FCA 472 (“Springfield Nominees”) said at [26] that ‘[f]or “special circumstances” to exist it is enough that there is a special feature of the case which affords a reason for modifying or releasing the undertaking and is not usually present.’
Whilst it is necessary that there be special circumstances, as the Full Court of the Federal Court said in Liberty Funding Pty Ltd v Phoenix Capital Ltd [2005] FCAFC 3 at [31]:
... The notion of “special circumstances” does not require that some extraordinary factors must bear on the question before the discretion will be exercised. It is sufficient to say that, in all the circumstances, good reason must be shown why, contrary to the usual position, documents produced or information obtained in one piece of litigation should be used for the advantage of a party in another piece of litigation or for other non-litigious purposes. The discretion is a broad one and all the circumstances of the case must be examined.
Although Wilcox J in Springfield Nominees indicated that it was neither possible nor desirable to prescribe an exhaustive list of factors that might lead the court to conclude that a party should be released from their implied obligation, his Honour did find that those factors would include the following matters:

... the nature of the document, the circumstances under which it came into existence, the attitude of the author of the document and any prejudice the author may sustain, whether the document pre-existed litigation or was created for that purpose and therefore expected to enter the public domain, the nature of the information in the document (in particular whether it contains personal data or commercially sensitive information), the circumstances in which the documents came into the hands of the applicant for leave and, perhaps most important of all, the likely contribution of the document to achieving justice in the second proceeding.
It is necessary to consider all of the circumstances in each case in order to determine whether there are special circumstances for releasing a party from the implied obligation.
Whether under the rule or on the application of Hearne v Street the issue in relation to the objected to documents, which appear highly personal in nature, is as to potential contribution to justice in the other proceedings.

THE MATERIAL

The suite of material is as follows:
(a) Notices of Risk filed by each party;

(b) Affidavits of the mother;

(c) Affidavits of the father;

(d) The three affidavits of Single Expert Dr B filed 18 November 2025;

(e) Judgment by her Honour Judge Costigan dated 20 January 2026 and interim orders;

(f) Section 67ZBE material produced by ACT Children, Youth and Families and Australian Capital Territory Police/Australian Federal Police;

(g) Recordings provided through disclosure;

(h) Documents produced by the mother by way of disclosure from Dr C;

(i) Documents produced by the mother by way of disclosure from Dr D;

(j) Documents produced by the mother by way of disclosure from Dr E;

(k) The unredacted component of material produced by F Organisation comprising employment records of the father;

(l) Material produced under the following subpoenas:

(i) H Church;

(ii) J Childhood Services;

(iii) G Organisation relating to the mother's employee personnel records (limited to emails to and from the mother regarding family violence issues from May 2025 onwards).

It may be observed that the material is wide ranging. A significant component of the material has been admitted in the proceedings already. The parties agree that the documents in categories (a), (b), (c) and (e) may be used in all the proceedings. There is otherwise incomplete agreement as set out in the table, including agreement that (f) and (g) be able to be used in the criminal and the family violence order proceedings, and that (d) be able to be used in the criminal proceedings.
The bulk of the remaining contention relates to treatment documents disclosed by the mother, the father’s employment records, material produced under subpoena by a church the parties attended, and a subpoena relating to the treatment of the children, but more broadly, whether much of the material may be used in the disciplinary proceedings.

DISCUSSION

A narrow dispute arose as to the mother using material in the disciplinary proceedings. While it appears that it is the mother’s allegations that are central to those proceedings, it has not been shown that the mother has a role to play in those proceedings, nor that she is an active protagonist in those proceedings. There is no justification for a release for the mother in relation to those proceedings.
As to the non-agreed part of the husband’s application, it should be acknowledged that aspects of the material to be used are highly personal to the mother, are produced by third parties, and may not be directly relevant to the family violence of sex assault allegations, in that they may not speak directly to those allegations.
However, the allegations sit within the broader context of the parties’ relationship, and the demise of that relationship. The material is sought in order to undermine the allegations made against the father. Whether this is in the form of a direct attack on the mother’s credibility, or a challenge to her evidence on the basis that it is inconsistent with, or incongruous with the material contained in the documents, it may be considered to be on the cards that the material may be of use in the proceedings. Whether that is because there is no mention of the allegations (understanding that there are many reasons why a person might not report sexual abuse or family violence at the time) or because it sits poorly with a description of the father or of the relationship proffered by the mother in other contexts, it is legitimate to have the use of the material for the purpose of challenging the allegations. This remains the case even for notes from therapeutic relationships, both for what was said or not said, and similarly representations made by the mother to her employer.
Similarly, representations made by the children or third parties to the expert or to others carry potential use, even if not admissibility, in the other proceedings. I doubt that the opinions offered by the expert or other therapists would be admissible in the other proceedings.
Whether any use of the material will be cogent to undermine the mother’s accounts of family violence or sexual assault is not able to be determined. Whether it will be admissible again is not known. The inability to answer such questions does not negative a genuine forensic need for the material for the above reasons.
The potential to contribute to the determination of each of the proceedings, as they turn on issues of the alleged sexual assaults, or family violence, means that, even if they form a small strand within that fabric, such is sufficient to justify the release.
Similarly, what has been represented by the father in his personnel file, or behaviour reported of him in that file (the mother referred to a clenching and unclenching of hands that she says is similar to what she experienced) may, or may not be cogent or admissible, but it is on the cards that it may assist the mother in her case in respect of the family violence proceedings. The mother sought access to that file, but also sought leave, in the event that the father otherwise received leave, in relation to Dr D and Dr E. Although Dr D, who produced material on subpoena, could be identified as the father’s psychiatrist, Dr E remained unknown. Although the mother referred to these as being agreed during submissions, they did not appear to be the subject of agreement in the schedule ultimately produced. It may then be that a further application will be forthcoming. However, given the nature of the issues in the case, the documents provided by Dr D, as the father’s psychiatrist, become relevant within the proceedings for the same reasons advanced in respect of the treating material of the mother, and should be released for the same reasons.
Although one way of dealing with the matter would be to deny permission regarding the material disclosed or produced on subpoena, leaving it for the parties to pursue a subpoena in the other proceedings, I am conscious of the additional litigation burden such a course would impose.

CONCLUSION

Orders will be made permitting the use of all the material in each of the proceedings. That permission will extend to allow the mother use of all of the material in the Magistrates Court proceedings to ensure equality of position between the parties in those proceedings. The nature of the mother’s use of material in the Supreme Court proceedings, in circumstances where she is both witness and putative victim but not a party, implies the use sought by her is for provision of such material to the Director of Public Prosecutions, and the order will reflect such. If that restriction does not reflect the extent of use sought, no doubt it will be the subject of further application. However, to the extent that the father calls upon the interests of justice to justify his use of the material, such interests also point to the mother providing such to those with the responsibility to prosecute the charges.
The possession of that material for use in the family violence and sexual assault proceedings will be in the hands of the lawyers who, as persons professionally rather than personally involved in the proceedings, and as bound by a strict ethical code, provide a safe holding of the documents.
The father sought a different arrangement for the use of documents in the disciplinary proceedings. He sought that, as he is unrepresented in those proceedings, he hold the documents.
In seeking such an arrangement he points to having been qualified to do so as being under the eye of the court and accordingly, a safe holder of the material.
However, he is in a very different position to professionally engaged lawyers. He is personally involved in the proceedings. He and the mother share, as observed by Judge Costigan, a highly conflicted relationship. Further, each of the father and mother is accused by the other of the use of the proceedings and the court mechanisms as instruments of abuse.
Given the sensitive nature of the material and the manner of its production to the court, it is appropriate that there be an arm’s length holding of the material.
While on the basis that he is self-represented the father says he should hold material for use with F Organisation, such an arrangement does not provide sufficient surety in the uncertain circumstances of this case. If the father wishes to use the documents with F Organisation he will need to do so via a solicitor, even if one is not engaged generally in those proceedings.
Orders will be made accordingly.
